elements.sent-2411-001: We show that deep neural networks achieve dimension-independent rates of convergence for learning structured densities such as those arising in image, audio, video, and text applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: More precisely, we demonstrate that neural networks with a simple $L^2$-minimizing loss achieve a rate of $n^{-1/(4+r)}$ in nonparametric density estimation when the underlying density is Markov to a graph whose maximum clique size is at most $r$, and we provide evidence that in the aforementioned applications, this size is typically constant, i.e., $r=O(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We then establish that the optimal rate in $L^1$ is $n^{-1/(2+r)}$ which, compared to the standard nonparametric rate of $n^{-1/(2+d)}$, reveals that the effective dimension of such problems is the size of the largest clique in the Markov random field.
elements.sent-2411-004: These rates are independent of the data's ambient dimension, making them applicable to realistic models of image, sound, video, and text data.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results provide a novel justification for deep learning's ability to circumvent the curse of dimensionality, demonstrating dimension-independent convergence rates in these contexts.
elements.sent-2411-006: Deep learning has emerged as a remarkably effective technique for numerous statistical problems that were historically extremely challenging, especially in high-dimensional settings.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the realm of deep generative models, which can be framed as density estimation, deep methods have showcased the ability to learn density functions with thousands or millions of dimensions using merely a few million data points \citep{oussidi18,ho20,cao24 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: This stands in stark contrast to standard density estimation theory, which would demand astronomical sample sizes due to the curse of dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-009: The manifold hypothesis is perhaps the most widely accepted explanation for deep learning's ability to circumvent this curse \citep{bengio13,brahma16 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This hypothesis posits that, despite a distribution's ambient space being high-dimensional, the mass of the density is heavily concentrated around a lower-dimensional subset of that space, such as an embedded manifold.
elements.sent-2411-011: As we will argue later, for complex data types of significant interest—images, video, sound, and text—this assumption is intimately linked to spatio-temporal locality.
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, covariates that are nearby spatio-temporally, e.g., neighboring pixels, tend to be strongly dependent, suggesting they lie near a lower-dimensional subspace.
elements.sent-2411-013: This paper investigates the benefits of leveraging the converse structure: The independence of spatio-temporally distant covariates.
elements.sent-2411-014: Covariates that are spatio-temporally distant often exhibit near-independence, particularly when conditioned on intervening covariates.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consider a sound recording: two one-second segments separated by a minute might share common elements, such as the same speaker.
elements.sent-2411-016: However, given the intervening minute of audio, these segments become effectively independent.
elements.sent-2411-017: The minute-long interval contains sufficient information to render the separated segments mutually uninformative.
elements.sent-2411-018: This principle of conditional independence extends to various data types, including images, where pixels far apart tend to be independent when conditioned on the surrounding region.
elements.sent-2411-019: This sort of dependence structure is naturally described by a Markov random field (MRF).
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we show that, for a very general class of densities that are Markov to an undirected graph (a.k.a.
elements.sent-2411-021: MRF), density estimation can be achieved with a neural network and a simple $L^2$ minimizing loss at a rate of approximately $n^{-1/(4+r)}$, where $r$ is the size of the largest clique in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-022: To compare, without the MRF assumption, the same class of densities can be estimated at a rate no better than $n^{-1/(2+d)}$, implying that the effective dimension is approximately $r$.
elements.sent-2411-023: We argue and show evidence that the MRF assumption is valid for many data types where neural networks excel (images, sound, video, etc.; see Figure \ref{fig:simple-graph-examples ) and demonstrate that this approach to density estimation causes the effective dimension $r$ of these problems to remain constant or at least be orders of magnitude smaller than the ambient dimension $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this section, we lay the foundation for our main results by introducing key concepts and related work.
elements.sent-2411-025: We begin by discussing traditional approaches to nonparametric density estimation and their limitations, particularly the curse of dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-026: We then explore the manifold hypothesis, a widely accepted explanation for the success of deep learning in high-dimensional settings.
elements.sent-2411-027: Following this, we introduce Markov random fields (MRFs) and their applications in modeling various types of data, including images and sequential information.
elements.sent-2411-028: This background will provide the necessary context for understanding the novelty of our approach, which leverages MRF structures to achieve dimension-independent convergence rates in density estimation, offering an alternative perspective to the manifold hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-029: Density estimation is the task of estimating a $d$-dimensional target probability density $p$ from observed data, $\rvx_1,\ldots,\rvx_n \iid p$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Of course, this is a classical problem for which we do not intend to provide a comprehensive overview, and instead refer readers to books such as \cite{devroye1985nonparametric,devroye01,tsybakov2009introduction for additional background.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Historically, $p$ was assumed to belong to a specific class of distributions, such as Gaussian, and the estimator $\hp_n$ was selected accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-032: For more complex $p$, nonparametric density estimators like kernel density estimators or histograms are employed \citep{devroye1985nonparametric,devroye01 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: These methods converge to $p$ for any density given sufficient data, but notably suffer from the curse of dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-034: For instance, when $p$ is Lipschitz continuous\footnote{A function $f:\R^d\to \R$ is Lipschitz continuous if there exists $L\ge 0$ such that $|f(x)-f(y)| \le L \left\|x-y\right\|_2$ for all $x,y$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: This nonparametric rate is known to be optimal for Lipschitz continuous densities, and numerous studies over the past decade have established that neural networks and generative models can achieve this optimal rate \citep{liang2017well,singh2018nonparametric, uppal2019nonparametric,oko2023diffusion,zhang2024minimax,kwon2024minimax .}
elements.sent-2411-036: This rate implies that the sample complexity grows exponentially in the dimension $d$, making the success of deep neural networks for estimating densities with millions of dimensions all the more remarkable.
elements.sent-2411-037: This is often explained via the manifold hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-038: The manifold hypothesis posits that many high-dimensional real-world distributions concentrate around lower-dimensional spaces, such as submanifolds of the ambient space.
elements.sent-2411-039: This assumption underpins, either explicitly or implicitly, numerous machine learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-040: For instance, principal component analysis assumes that a distribution is concentrated around an affine subspace, while sparsity assumptions can be formulated as a union of manifolds, where the subsets of the union are axis-aligned subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-041: The success of deep learning methods in handling high-dimensional data, such as images, videos, and audio, is frequently attributed to the manifold hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-042: Experimental validation of the manifold hypothesis has been conducted for image datasets.
elements.sent-2411-043: In \cite{pope21 , the authors determined that the intrinsic dimension of the ImageNet dataset lies between 25 and 40 dimensions, significantly lower than its ambient dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This hypothesis is closely linked to correlation and dependency between covariates.
elements.sent-2411-045: In images, for example, adjacent pixels $\rx_i$ and $\rx_j$ typically have similar values, causing the dataset to concentrate towards the linear subspace $\rx_i=\rx_j$, which is a submanifold.
elements.sent-2411-046: Figure \ref{fig:scatterplots (a) illustrates this concept, showing the values of pixels $(8,8)$ and $(8,9)$ for 100 randomly selected images from the grayscaled CIFAR-10 dataset, where a strong concentration along the diagonal is evident.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Further supporting this hypothesis, \cite{carlsson08 discovered that the set of $3\times3$ pixel patches from natural images concentrates around a 2-dimensional manifold.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Theoretically, distributions concentrating around lower-dimensional subsets of the ambient space have been shown to yield improved estimation properties.
elements.sent-2411-049: For instance, \cite{weed19 demonstrated that while a dataset typically converges at rate $n^{-1/d}$ to the true distribution in Wasserstein distance, when the dataset exhibits a lower $d'$-dimensional structure, it converges at the faster rate of $n^{-1/d'}$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Similar results illustrating the manifold hypothesis and its benefits can be found in \citet{pelletier2005kernel,ozakin2009submanifold,jiang2017kde,schmidt2019manifold,nakada2020adaptive,berenfeld2022estimating,jiao2023deep,tang2024adaptivity .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Another line of related work uses Barron functions, which are a class of functions inspired by neural networks, to achieve dimension-free rates \citep{barron1993universal,klusowski2018approximation,ma2022barron,cole2024score , in contrast to our use of weaker Lipschitz-type assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-052: While the manifold hypothesis explains local dependencies, it's worth considering scenarios that deviate from this model.
elements.sent-2411-053: For example, the manifold hypothesis cannot be satisfied when covariates are independent.
elements.sent-2411-054: For example, if two covariates $\rx\sim p_{\rx}$ and $\ry \sim p_{\ry}$ are independent, their joint density $p_{\rx,\ry (x,y) = p_\rx(x)p_\ry(y)$ fills a rectangle in their product space.}
elements.sent-2411-055: As one may expect, pixels that are distant from one another tend to become more independent.
elements.sent-2411-056: This phenomenon is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:scatterplots (d), which plots the grayscale values of pixels $(8,8)$ and $(14,28)$, showing a more dispersed pattern.}
elements.sent-2411-057: This observation naturally leads to modeling the space of images as a Markov random field, where local dependencies are captured while allowing for independence between distant pixels.
elements.sent-2411-058: A Markov random field (MRF) consists of a random vector $\rvx = (\ervx_1,\ldots,\ervx_d)$ and a graph $\gG=(V,E)$, where the graph's vertices correspond to the entries of the random vector, i.e., $V = \{\ervx_1,\ldots,\ervx_d\}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: The graph encodes information about the conditional independence of the vector's entries.
elements.sent-2411-061: Given three disjoint subsets $A,B,C$ of $\{\ervx_1,\ldots,\ervx_d\}$, the graph $\gG$ indicates that the random vectors $\rvx_A$ and $\rvx_B$ are conditionally independent given $\rvx_C$ if there is no path from $A$ to $B$ that doesn't pass through $C$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Consider a simple example with random variables $\rx$, $\ry$, and $\rz$, where $\rx = \ry + \epsilon_\rx$ and $\rz = \ry + \epsilon_\rz$, with $\epsilon_\rx$, $\epsilon_\rz$, and $\ry$ being jointly independent.
elements.sent-2411-063: In this scenario, the distributions of $\rx$ and $\rz$ are conditionally independent given $\ry$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Figure \ref{fig:simple-mrf illustrates the corresponding MRF graph for this example.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The random variables $\rx$ and $\ry$ are independent given $\rz$.
elements.sent-2411-066: In other words, covariates can be conditionally independent in reality even if this independence is not explicitly represented in the MRF structure.
elements.sent-2411-067: The MRF provides a conservative model of independence relationships, capturing known or assumed conditional independencies without ruling out additional independencies that may exist in the data.
elements.sent-2411-068: Consequently, any random vector associated with a complete graph—where all vertices are adjacent to one another—is a valid MRF, since it provides no information about the independence of the covariates.
elements.sent-2411-069: This is because every vertex is connected to every other vertex, so removing any number of vertices will never separate the graph into multiple components.
elements.sent-2411-070: Because it conveys no information about the conditional independence of the covariates, it even applies to a random vector where all entries are independent.
elements.sent-2411-071: One of the most well-known MRFs is the Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-072: A Markov chain of length $d$ corresponds to the ``path'' graph $L_d$ of $d$ random variables.
elements.sent-2411-073: The above example with $\rx,\ry,\rz$ corresponds to the graph $L_3$ and the MRF corresponding to $L_4$ shown in Figure \ref{fig:simple-graph-examples .}
elements.sent-2411-074: In a Markov chain, the indices are often interpreted as a time parameter.
elements.sent-2411-075: A classic example is a gambling scenario: A person's money at time $t+1$ is conditionally independent of their total value at time $t-s$ (for $s>0$), given their value at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-076: This property, known as the Markov property, encapsulates the idea that the future state depends only on the present state, not on past states.
elements.sent-2411-077: Markov chains have a long history of use for modeling sequential information, including audio and text data.

elements.sent-2411-085: This approach assumes that each pixel is best predicted using just its neighbors and ignoring the rest of the image.

elements.section-2411-003: Improving The Path and Grid Markov Random Field Models
elements.sent-2411-088: While standard path and grid MRF models may suffice for correcting extremely local or high-frequency noise in sequential or spatial data, they fall far short of capturing the true distribution of complex data types.
elements.sent-2411-089: Consider, for example, audio data consisting of 21-second clips where the middle second is missing and needs to be predicted.
elements.sent-2411-090: According to the path MRF model, this missing second would depend solely on the audio samples directly preceding and following it.
elements.sent-2411-091: Consequently, under a Markov chain (i.e. path MRF) model, the remaining $20$ seconds of audio (less two samples) would be deemed completely uninformative for predicting the middle second, given these two adjacent samples.
elements.sent-2411-092: This simplistic model fails to capture the richer, longer-range dependencies present in real-world audio data.
elements.sent-2411-093: In practice, the content of the missing second is likely influenced by a broader context than just its immediate neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-094: For instance, the rhythm or theme established in the preceding few seconds, or the anticipation of what follows immediately after, could be crucial for predicting the missing segment.
elements.sent-2411-095: This moderately broader context is entirely discarded by the basic path MRF.
elements.sent-2411-096: Similarly, for image data, the standard grid MRF model suggests that a region of an image depends only on its immediate bordering pixels.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, realistic images often exhibit patterns and structures that span multiple pixels in various directions.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, the edge of an object or a gradient in lighting might extend across several pixels, creating dependencies that the basic grid model fails to capture.
elements.sent-2411-099: Figure \ref{fig:width-comparison illustrates this concept concretely, demonstrating the effects of different MRF models on image inpainting tasks and highlighting the implications of varying levels of contextual information.}
elements.sent-2411-100: These limitations motivate the need for more sophisticated MRF models where segments or regions are more extensively connected, allowing for the incorporation of relevant contextual information without necessarily spanning the entire dataset.

Modeling this image with an MRF graph $L_{640 \times 427}$ or $L^+_{640 \times 427}$ would imply that the distribution of the missing interior in (d) depends exclusively on its 1-pixel wide border, with the rest of the image in (a) being uninformative for predicting this interior region. In contrast, predicting the interior using the 12-pixel border in (c) is more reasonable. This scenario corresponds to models like $L_{640 \times 427}^6$ or $\left(L^+_{640 \times 427}\right)^6$, which capture more extensive local dependencies. It's important to note that for the MRF model to hold, the interior doesn't need to be deterministically constructed from the surrounding pixels. Rather, the surrounding pixels need only provide sufficient information about the interior (e.g., that it's a cat's face) such that the rest of the image doesn't contribute any additional information for predicting the interior region.} \label{fig:width-comparison}
elements.sent-2411-101: To model sequential and spatial data more realistically, we propose using the ``power graph'' of the path and grid models.
elements.sent-2411-102: For a graph $\gG$, the power graph $\gG^t$ with $t \in \N$ is defined as the graph where an edge exists between every pair of vertices within $t$ steps of each other in $\gG$, with $\gG^1 = \gG$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Figures \ref{fig:path-power and \ref{fig:grid-power illustrate this concept using path graphs and grid graphs, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: This construction causes contiguous sections of sequences and patches of grids to become fully connected, as demonstrated in Figure \ref{fig:path-power .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Applying this power graph concept to a grid graph assumes that local patches of images are highly dependent, making no assumptions about conditional independence within a patch.
elements.sent-2411-106: It also implies that distant regions of an image become independent as the distance between them increases, and that these regions are independent when conditioned on a sufficiently wide separating region of pixels.
elements.sent-2411-107: We can experimentally validate these assumptions using image data.
elements.sent-2411-108: The top row of Figure \ref{fig:scatterplots shows the grayscale values of pixel (8,8) versus selected other pixels for 100 randomly chosen images from the CIFAR-10 training dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The bottom row repeats this experiment, but conditioned on the value of the adjacent pixel (9,8) being near its median value.
elements.sent-2411-110: These experiments reveal that, when conditioned on the adjacent pixel, the dependence (as measured by correlation) decreases significantly.
elements.sent-2411-111: Notably, pixels (8,8) and (9,12) appear almost completely independent when conditioned on pixel (9,8).
elements.sent-2411-112: This provides strong evidence for the validity of the MRF model.
elements.sent-2411-113: The MRF model predicts that (8,8) and (9,12) should be independent when conditioned on surrounding pixels (the number of which depends on the graph power of the MRF graph).
elements.sent-2411-114: Remarkably, we observe that pixels appear independent when conditioned on just a single adjacent pixel, suggesting that the grid MRF assumption may be even more conservative than necessary.
elements.sent-2411-115: The power graph extension of path and grid MRFs presents a fundamentally different perspective on modeling high-dimensional data compared to the widely accepted manifold hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-116: While the manifold hypothesis posits that high-dimensional data concentrates around lower-dimensional structures, our MRF approach embraces the full dimensionality of the data, focusing instead on the independence structure between variables.
elements.sent-2411-117: This model aligns well with the observed structure in various data types, capturing local dependencies while allowing for long-range independencies.
elements.sent-2411-118: For sequential data such as audio or text, it accounts for strong dependencies between nearby elements while acknowledging the decreasing influence of distant context.
elements.sent-2411-119: In spatial data like images, it models high correlation between neighboring pixels and gradual decorrelation as distance increases.
elements.sent-2411-120: Our experimental results provide compelling evidence for this MRF model's validity.
elements.sent-2411-121: The observed conditional independence between distant pixels, given intervening pixels, supports our power graph MRF approach's fundamental assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-122: It's important to note that this approach is not meant to supersede the manifold hypothesis, but instead to augment it.
elements.sent-2411-123: The manifold hypothesis explains sample efficiency from local structure, while the MRF model adds additional model efficiency from a global perspective.
elements.sent-2411-124: Together, they provide a more comprehensive framework for understanding high-dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-125: Remarkably, in the following section, we will demonstrate that under these MRF assumptions, there exist estimators based on neural networks with standard loss functions (e.g. squared loss) that can achieve dimension-independent rates of convergence for density estimation.
elements.sent-2411-126: This result is particularly significant as it suggests a path to overcoming the curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional density estimation tasks without relying on low-dimensional embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-127: By focusing on independence structures rather than dimension reduction, our approach offers a novel explanation for the success of deep learning methods in processing complex, high-dimensional data, complementing and contrasting with the insights provided by the manifold hypothesis.
elements.section-2411-004: MRF-Based Density Estimation with Neural Networks
elements.sent-2411-128: We begin by presenting the foundational results of this work that demonstrate that one can estimate a density $p$ given its Markov graph, at a rate that depends only on the size of the largest clique of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-129: We will present two results, one using a neural network style architecture using a practical empirical risk minimization style training and a second estimator that is more complex and computationally intractable that achieves approximately optimal rates of convergence.
elements.sent-2411-130: Our estimators are based on the classical Hammersley-Clifford Theorem \citep{hammersley1971markov .}
elements.sent-2411-131: Before presenting the theorem we must review a few concepts.
elements.sent-2411-132: A graph $\gG$ is called complete if every vertex is adjacent to every other vertex.
elements.sent-2411-133: For a graph $\gG=(V,E)$ a clique is a complete subgraph, i.e., $\gG' = (V',E')$ with $V' \subset V$ and $E'\subset E$, that is complete.
elements.sent-2411-134: A maximal clique of a graph is a set of cliques which are not contained within another clique.
elements.sent-2411-135: Observe that maximal cliques of the same graph can have different numbers of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-136: See Figure \ref{fig:cliques-example for examples of maximal cliques.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The collection of the maximal cliques of a graph will be denoted $\maxcliques(\gG)$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Let $\maxcliques(\gG)$ be the set of maximal cliques in $\gG$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Then \begin{equation}p(\vx) = \prod_{V' \in \maxcliques(\gG)} \psi_{V'}(\vx_{V'}),\end{equation} where $\vx_{V'}$ are the indices of $\vx$ corresponding to $V'$.
elements.sent-2411-140: The weights are constrained to $[-1,1]$, effectively implementing weight decay via constrained optimization rather than norm penalization.
elements.sent-2411-141: We analyze an estimator that minimizes the integrated squared error between $p$ and our estimator $\hp$: \begin{equation}\int \left(p\left(\vx\right) - \hp\left(\vx\right)\right)^2 d\vx = \int p(\vx)^2 d\vx - 2 \int p(\vx)\hp(\vx) d\vx + \int \hp(\vx)^2 d\vx. \label{eqn:l2-loss-expand}\end{equation} In empirical minimization, the first term of \eqref{eqn:l2-loss-expand is constant.}

elements.sent-2411-175: The MRF approach can be extended to various data types, yielding similar dimension reduction results.
elements.sent-2411-176: For instance, color images can be modeled as a three-dimensional random tensor $\tX \in \R^{c\times w\times h}$ with a graph $\gG$.
elements.sent-2411-177: In this model, the vertices in $\tX_{:,i,j}\cup\tX_{:,i',j'}$ are fully connected for $|i-i'| \le 1$ and $|j-j'| \le 1$, corresponding to a grid graph with diagonals where all channels are connected.
elements.sent-2411-178: Video data can be represented by four-dimensional graphs corresponding to order-4 tensors in $\R^{t\times c \times w \times h}$, with a similar connective structure.
elements.sent-2411-179: While text data is discrete in nature, once tokenized and passed through $d$-dimensional word embeddings, it resembles spatial data with dimensions $\R^{d\times t}$ and can benefit from independence structure.
elements.sent-2411-180: In all these cases, the maximum clique size is determined by how quickly independence is achieved spatio-temporally or in the embedding space, rather than by the overall data dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-181: This approach yields effective dimensions that are orders of magnitude smaller than the ambient dimension, leading to dimension-independent learning rates.
elements.sent-2411-182: Crucially, this dimension independence is maintained across varying data sizes.
elements.sent-2411-183: For instance, cropping an image would leave the maximum clique size unchanged (provided the cropping isn't too extreme), while expanding an image would create a larger MRF graph but, assuming the underlying pattern holds, the maximum clique size would remain constant.
elements.sent-2411-184: This property results in a dimension-independent rate of learning that remains consistent across different image sizes.
elements.sent-2411-185: Thus, whether dealing with a $100\times 100$ pixel image or a $1000 \times 1000$ pixel image of similar content, the effective learning rate remains tied to the maximum clique size rather than the total number of pixels, exemplifying true dimension independence in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-186: These extensions demonstrate the versatility of the MRF approach in modeling complex, high-dimensional data structures across various modalities, while significantly reducing the effective dimensionality of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-187: Although not the primary focus of this work, our results have potential applications to other data types not typically associated with deep learning.

elements.sent-2411-192: Estimating densities with a tree MRF has been studied previously and is called ``tree density estimation.'' The largest clique size being 2 and yielding a $\tO(n^{-1/4 )$ rate of convergence approximately matches previous work on this problem.}

elements.sent-2411-196: Although not the primary focus of this work, we present the following result which approximately matches (up to $\log$ terms) the best possible rate for MRFs.

elements.sent-2411-202: Neural density estimation has been the subject of intense study over the past few decades, dating at least back to \cite{magdon1998neural .}
elements.sent-2411-203: There has recently been interest in designing structured neural density estimators that exploit graphical structure \citep{germain2015made,johnson2016composing,khemakhem2021causal,wehenkel2021graphical,chen2024structured .}
elements.sent-2411-204: In this work, we have presented a novel perspective on the success of neural networks in density estimation problems.
elements.sent-2411-205: Our approach, based on Markov Random Field (MRF) structures, offers an alternative explanation to the widely accepted manifold hypothesis for why deep learning methods can circumvent the curse of dimensionality, and aligns with these recent developments on structured density estimation.
elements.sent-2411-206: We have demonstrated that leveraging MRF assumptions can achieve dimension-independent convergence rates for density estimation, with the effective dimensionality determined by the largest clique in the MRF graph rather than the ambient data dimension.
elements.sent-2411-207: This potentially explains the efficacy of neural networks in domains like image and sequential data processing.
elements.sent-2411-208: Our MRF-based approach complements, rather than replaces, the manifold hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-209: We envision a combination of local manifold-like structures and global MRF-like independence properties at play in real-world scenarios, with the manifold hypothesis explaining local features and our MRF approach capturing broader independence structures.
elements.sent-2411-210: This work opens avenues for future research, including investigating the interplay between local manifold structures and global MRF properties, and developing practical algorithms exploiting these structures within deep learning frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-211: In conclusion, our work provides a novel theoretical framework for understanding neural networks in high-dimensional spaces, offering an alternative to the manifold hypothesis and potentially stimulating new directions in both the theory and practice of machine learning for high-dimensional tasks.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
